coredata.dc:title: Probing glutathione reductase activity with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles system
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiaolu
Zhao, Xian-En
Sun, Jing
Zhu, Shuyun
Lei, Cuihua
Li, Ru
Gong, Peiwei
Ling, Baoping
Wang, Renjun
Wang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were combined for probing glutathione reductase (GR) activity. The main probing mechanism is based on the following facts: (1) The fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched by AuNPs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). (2) Reduced glutathione (GSH) can protect AuNPs from being aggregated and enlarge the inter-particle distance between GQDs and AuNPs, which subsequently produces the fluorescent signal recovery. (3) AuNPs has much weaker affinity towards oxidized glutathione (GSSG) in contrast to GSH. (4) GR can catalyze the reduction of GSSG into GSH in the presence of coenzyme β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phophate hydrate (NADPH). Based on these facts, a turn-on fluorescent assay for GR has thus been developed based on the GQDs-AuNPs system. The proposed assay can allow for the evaluation of GR activity in the range of 0.0050–0.13 mU/mL, with a minimum detectable concentration of 0.0050 mU/mL, which was more than one order of magnitude lower than most of the current sensing platforms. Besides, this GQDs-AuNPs based GR assay was successfully applied for screening the inhibitor of GR activity by using 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU) as a model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Enzyme catalysis
Glutathione reductase
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Main principle of the evaluation of GR activity
Detection of GR in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic principle of GR detection using GQDs-AuNPs system.
(A) The low-magnification and (B) the high-magnification TEM images of GQDs, (C) the AFM image of GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs and AuNPs; and emission spectrum of GQDs. (B,C) XPS spectra of GQDs: high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B) and high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (C). (D) FTIR spectra of GQDs and AuNPs.
TEM images of AuNPs alone (A), the aggregated AuNPs induced by GQDs (B), the dispersed AuNPs in the presence of GQDs and GSH (C), and the aggregated AuNPs in the presence of GQDs and GSSG (D).

(A) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of GQDs among the presence of different compositions: (a) GQDs alone, (b) GQDs with AuNPs, (c) GQDs with AuNPs and GSH, (d) GQDs with AuNPs and GSSG. (B) Fluorescence intensities of GQDs-AuNPs (1), and GQDs-AuNPs in the presence of 240 μM GSSG (2), 40 μM NADPH (3), 0.10 mU/mL GR (4), 240 μM GSSG with 40 μM NADPH (5), 240 μM GSSG with 0.10 mU/mL GR (6), 40 μM NADPH with 0.10 mU/mL GR (7), GSH (8), and 240 μM GSSG, 40 μM NADPH with 0.10 mU/mL GR (9).

The effects of enzyme reaction time (A) and pH (B) on the enhancement factor (F-F 0)/F 0, where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-AuNPs system in the presence and absence of GR, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-AuNPs system in the presence of different GR concentrations (from bottom: 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10, and 0.13 mU/mL). (B) Enhancement factors (F-F 0)/F 0 versus the concentrations of GR. F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of GQDs in the absence and presence of GR at 525 nm, respectively; [GSSG] = 240 μM, [NADPH] = 40 μM.

Changes of fluorescence intensities of GQDs-AuNPs system caused by various proteins in the presence of 240 μM GSSG and 40 μM NADPH. The concentrations of these proteins are 1.2 μg/mL with the exception of [GR] = 0.10 mU/mL (∼0.12 μg/mL).
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the determination of GR.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Two molecules of fluorescein joined by a short disulfide-containing linker.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of GR activity in human serum.

head.ce:title: Probing glutathione reductase activity with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles system

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A turn-on fluorescent nanosensor was developed for probing glutathione reductase (GR) activity based on the FRET between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were combined for probing glutathione reductase (GR) activity. The main probing mechanism is based on the following facts: (1) The fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched by AuNPs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). (2) Reduced glutathione (GSH) can protect AuNPs from being aggregated and enlarge the inter-particle distance between GQDs and AuNPs, which subsequently produces the fluorescent signal recovery. (3) AuNPs has much weaker affinity towards oxidized glutathione (GSSG) in contrast to GSH. (4) GR can catalyze the reduction of GSSG into GSH in the presence of coenzyme β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phophate hydrate (NADPH). Based on these facts, a turn-on fluorescent assay for GR has thus been developed based on the GQDs-AuNPs system. The proposed assay can allow for the evaluation of GR activity in the range of 0.0050–0.13 mU/mL, with a minimum detectable concentration of 0.0050 mU/mL, which was more than one order of magnitude lower than most of the current sensing platforms. Besides, this GQDs-AuNPs based GR assay was successfully applied for screening the inhibitor of GR activity by using 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU) as a model.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme plays a crucial role in the process of biocatalytic transformation. Advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology have resulted in various nanosensors for monitoring of biocatalysis events [1,2]. Recently, various fluorescent nanomaterials have been growingly utilized for probing enzyme bioanalysis through the luminescence detection of biorecognition events and biocatalytic processes [3–5]. For example, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and noble metal nanoclusters have been used to follow the enzymatic processes [6–8]. However, both the two fluorescent nanomaterials might suffer from some drawbacks, such as laborious synthesis procedure, heavy metals potential toxicity and environmental hazards for semiconductor QDs, and high cost for metal nanoclusters. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop new fluorescent probes for probing the enzymatic catalysis activities and processes.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new class of zero-dimensional graphic nanomaterials with lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm in a single layer, double layer, and multi-layers (3–<10) [9]. With respect to conventional semiconductor QDs and noble metal nanoclusters, GQDs are superior in terms of cytotoxicity, photostability, water solubility, and photoluminescence profiles, which make them quite promising in sensors, bioimaging [10], optoelectronic devices [11] and so on. To this end, GQDs have been considered as a low-toxicity, eco-friendly candidate of fluorescent sensor materials [12,13]. So far, various analytes, such as metal ions [14,15], glucose [16], biological small molecules [17–19], and DNA [20,21] have been assayed based on GQDs sensors. However, the development and application of GQDs in fluorometric assays is still in its initial stage, and especially very few works have been done for probing biocatalytic transformations. Up to date, the biocatalytic processes involving trypsin [22], acetylcholinesterase [23], and tyrosinase [24] have been probed by GQDs.

Glutathione reductase (GR), as the β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phophate hydrate (NADPH)-dependent enzyme can catalyze the reduction of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) to reduced glutathione (GSH). GSH plays an essential role as a major antioxidant including the protection of cells against various oxidative stresses, xenobiotic metabolism, intra-cellular signal transduction, and gene regulation [25]. Thus, GR is known to play crucial role in response to some diseases and conditions such as the genesis of anxiety and biological oxidative stress [26]. In addition, it has been reported that a significant enhancement of the GR activity was observed in malignant tumor cell [27]. Hence, monitoring the activity of GR in physiological media is of great importance.

The classic spectrophotometric methods for the determination of GR activity are based on the changes of NADPH or DTNB, although still widely used, are mostly insensitive and vulnerable [28,29]. Alternatively, the fluorimetric analysis methods have been recognized to present the higher sensitivity in monitoring the GR activities. For example, A.M. Piggott et al. applied a new fluorescence probe consisting of two molecules of fluorescein joined by a short disulfide-containing linker (FSSF) to the determination of GR activity [30]. Fan et al. developed a highly sensitive fluorescence turn on assay for GR by taking advantages of the distinct fluorescence rates of anionic conjugated polyelectrolyte caused by GSH and GSSG [31]. With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, recently, some fluorescent nanoprobes have been used for the determination of GR activity such as CdS QDs [32], DNA templated silver nanoclusters [33], and DNA bio-dots [34]. However, most of these fluorophores might suffer from some drawbacks, such as poor photostability and solubility in water for FSSF, laborious synthesis procedure for fluorescent polyelectrolyte and CdS QDs, high-cost for silver nanoclusters and DNA bio-dots. In our previous work, we have developed a sensitive fluorescent detection assay for probing GR activity based on the recovered fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-Hg(II) system [35]. However, this fluorescent assay is not environmentally friendly due to the usage of heavy metal Hg(II). In addition, the fluorescent CQDs has an emission maximum of 434 nm, and hence, the assay is susceptible to interference from autofluorescence and from the inherent fluorescence of NADPH, which is an integral part of the assay. Thus, it is still highly desirable to develop new fluorescent assay for probing GR activity.

In the present study, we have developed a sensitive and selective probing system for the evaluation GR catalysis activities based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the GQDs (fluorophore) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (absorber). As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the addition of GQDs into AuNPs colloid solution might induce the aggregation of AuNPs and GQDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs at 532 nm through a FRET quenching mechanism. GR can catalyze the reduction of GSSG back to GSH in the presence of NADPH and the yielding GSH can enlarge the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and GQDs followed by a recovery of the fluorescence of GQDs. By taking the advantages of these unique features, a GQDs-AuNPs based “turn-on” FRET fluorescent biosensor has thus been proposed for the first time for evaluating the GR activity. The proposed sensing strategy provided the exciting detection limit of 0.0050 mU/mL (S/N = 3), which was 1–2 orders of magnitude lower than most of the recently reported sensing platforms. This developed assay is simple and environmentally friendly avoiding the intricate preparation, surface modification, and usage of heavy metal. More importantly, the longer-wavelength emission of GQDs can eliminate the potential interference from NADPH. In addition, it can allow for the screening of the inhibitors of GR.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were obtained from XFNANO. Glutathione reductase (GR) from baker’s yeast, reduced glutathione (GSH, 98%), oxidized glutathione (GSSG, 98%), β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phosphate reduced tetrasodium salt hydrate (NADPH, 95%), gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (C6H11Na3O7·2H2O), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trypsin, glucose oxidase (GOx), myoglobin, thrombin, amylase, ethanol dehydrogenase (ADH), and glutathione reductase assay kit (GRSA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Acrylamide, cysteine (Cys), and homocysteine (HCys) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade, and used as received. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of various pH were prepared with different ratios of Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, and ultrapure water. The stock solutions of GR and NADPH were both freshly prepared and cooled on ice before use.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were captured using a Multi Mode Nanoscope IIIa controller atomic force microscope (Veeco, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed by a PHI 5702 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy experiments were performed on a Magna-IR 380 (Nicolet). A Nano-ZS Zeta sizer ZEN3600 (Malvern, U.K.) was used to measure the zeta potential of GQDs and AuNPs. All fluorescence measurements were carried out on a fluorescence spectrometer (Max-4, HORIBA, USA) operated at an excitation wavelength at 467 nm, with both excitation and emission slit widths of 5.0 nm. The fluorescence intensities were collected at 532 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600, Japan). The desired pH buffer solutions were adjusted with pHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai, China).

The colloidal solution of citrate-stabilized AuNPs was prepared using Frens’ method described in a previous report [36]. Briefly, 120 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution was heated to boiling under vigorous stirring. Then, rapid addition of 12 mL of 38.8 mM trisodium citrate solution to the vortex of the solution resulted in a color change from pale yellow to wine red. The reaction was allowed to continue for another 20 min. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature. The as-prepared AuNPs were stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator for further use. The molar extinction coefficient of AuNPs at 522 nm for 13 nm spherical AuNPs is about 2.7 × 108 M−1 cm−1 L−1, thus the concentration of the obtained AuNPs was calculated to 10.58 nM according to Lambert Beer’s law [37].

For the evaluation of GR activity, 120 μL GSSG (1.0 mM), 20 μL NADPH (1 mM), and 10 μL GR of different concentrations were mixed with 0.441 nM AuNPs in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 6.0) for 15 min. Then, 30 μL of 1.0 mg mL−1 GQDs were added to the reaction solution under gentle shaking with the total volume of 500 μL. After incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

Furthermore, the selectivity of this assay was evaluated in the presence of other biological proteins and enzymes including BSA, trypsin, GOx, myoglobin, thrombin, amylase, and ADH. The inhibitor experiments were carried out according to the same procedure mentioned above, except that GR was pre-incubated with BCNU for 10 min.

The morphology of GQDs was characterized using TEM and AFM imaging. As shown in Fig. 1 A, GQDs were uniform with the average size of 3.0-5.0 nm in diameter. In addition, the TEM image reveals that GQDs were well-dispersed in water and well-separated from each other, as evidenced by the high-magnification image (Fig. 1B). Furthermore, the AFM imaging was performed for the GQD assemblies (Fig. 1C), showing the heights were between 1.1 and 3.2 nm that can correspond to 1–3 graphene layers, which is consistent to those previously reported [38,39].

The optical properties of GQDs and AuNPs were examined. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the GQDs suspension showed the UV–vis absorption peak around 230 nm, which is attributable to the π-π* transition [40]. The fluorescence emissions of GQDs with different excitation wavelengths were also investigated. Like most of fluorescent carbon nanodots, GQDs also exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. With increasing excitation wavelengths, the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelengths and then the intensity decreased rapidly. Such a phenomenon has also been observed from other-based quantum dots, which may arise from the different particle size and different emission sites of the formed GQDs [41,42]. The maximum emission intensity for GQDs was achieved at 532 nm when excited at 467 nm. AuNPs absorption spectrum demonstrated a strong characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak at 522 nm and a significant overlap with GQDs emission spectrum (Fig. 2A).

The surface composition and elemental analysis of GQDs were carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full range XPS analysis (Fig. S1) showed that the GQDs sample was composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, which appeared at 284.9, 400.2, and 531.5 eV, respectively. In the expanded XPS spectra, the high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) showed four peaks centered at 284.2, 285.1, 286.2, 287.3, and 288.1 eV, demonstrating the presence of CC, CC, CO, CN, and CO functional groups [43]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2C) showed the strong signals from both amide-N and doping N atoms, which were further confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 2D). The FTIR spectra of GQDs exhibited characteristic absorption bands of ν(CN) 1389 cm−1 and ν(NH) 3441 cm−1, indicating the presence of amine groups on the surface of GQDs. Surface properties of the as-synthesized AuNPs were also investigated using FTIR spectra, in which characteristic absorption bands of ν(OH) and ν(COO−) at 3450 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 were observed, indicating the existence of carboxylic groups on the surface of as-prepared AuNPs (Fig. 2D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main principle of the evaluation of GR activity
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we explored the underlying quenching mechanism between GQDs and AuNPs. The zeta potential of the GQDs and AuNPs were measured to be −31.4 and −32.2 mV, respectively (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). There was an electrostatic repulsive between the negatively charged GQDs and negatively charged AuNPs. However, the investigations on the surface properties of GQDs and AuNPs revealed that there were amino groups on the surface of GQDs and carboxylic groups on the surface of AuNPs (Fig. 2C,D). Therefore, the possible interaction between the N atom on the surface of GQDs and AuNPs and the possible electron interaction between GQDs and AuNPs might take place. In addition, there is a good spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and emission spectrum of GQDs. Therefore, the addition of GQDs into AuNPs colloids solution might induce the aggregation of AuNPs and GQDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs through a FRET quenching mechanism. However, GSH showed a strong affinity to AuNPs due to the multi-dentate anchor, thus rendering the encapsulation of AuNPs by GSH in priority [44]. As a result, the presence of GSH can enlarge the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and GQDs followed by a recovery of the fluorescence of GQDs. In contrast, GQDs and AuNPs would still aggregate together with the fluorescence quenching of GQDs, since GSSG possesses a weak affinity with AuNPs. This hypothesis was also proved by TEM imaging shown in Fig. 3 . Obviously, the mixture of GQDs and AuNPs did result in their aggregation (Fig. 3A and B). GSH could reverse the process from aggregation to dispersion state (Fig. 3C), whereas GSSG could not reverse the process from the aggregation to the dispersion state (Fig. 3D).

Based on these facts, a turn-on fluorescent biosensor has thus been developed for the evaluation of GR activity and screening of its inhibitors. As shown in Scheme 1, the introduction of GR, GSSG, and NADPH into the GQDs-AuNPs system brought about the substantial fluorescence recovery, owing to the fact that GR could catalyze GSSG to GSH in the presence of NADPH. The enhanced fluorescence intensities could be correlated to varying GR levels to expect the feasible evaluation of GR activity.

The practical feasibility of GQDs for the qualitative evaluation of GR activity was first assessed with the results shown in Fig. 4 A. It was noted that GQDs themselves exhibited a strong fluorescence (curve a) that could be quenched about 40% fluorescence intensity in the presence of AuNPs (curve b). While GSH was added into the GQDs-AuNPs system, about 93% fluorescence intensity was recovered due to the strong coordination between Au and sulfur atoms (curve c). However, when GSSG was added into the GQDs-AuNPs system, a slight change in fluorescence intensity was observed due to the weak interaction between AuNPs and GSSG (curve d). The results indicated that the GQDs-AuNPs system can possess the different recognition abilities towards GSH and GSSG, thus showing the potential for the evaluation of GR activity.

Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-AuNPs system was investigated under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 4B, the florescence intensities of GQDs-AuNPs system changed negligibly in the presence of GSSG, NADPH, GSSG with NADPH, GSSG with GR, or GR with NADPH. In contrast, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs-AuNPs system increased obviously in the presence of GSH or GSSG, GR with coenzyme NADPH. Accordingly, this fluorimetric sensor could probe the enzyme catalysis transformation from GSSG to GSH in the presence of GR. Therefore, the activities of GR could be readily monitored by recording the changes of GQD fluorescence intensity.

Main reaction conditions including the time and medium pH were optimized to gain the best results. Firstly, we investigated the reaction time of AuNPs and GSH. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-AuNPs in the presence of GSH increased quickly with the increasing time from 1 min to 5 min and then changed slightly when the time is more than 5 min. Consequently, 5 min is adopted as the optimal time for the reaction between AuNPs and GSH.

The enzyme reaction time is an important factor for the detection system since it is directly related with the amounts of the yielded GSH. The enzyme reaction time was investigated ranging from 1 min to 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the enhancement factor (F-F 0)/F 0, where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-AuNPs system in the presence and absence of GR, respectively, increased firstly with the increasing enzyme reaction time from 1 min to 15 min and then stayed unchanged when the time is more than 15 min. As a result, 15 min was chosen as the optimal enzyme reaction time.

The choice of pH values is also a crucial factor for the evaluation of GR activity. Thus, the effects of pH ranging from 4.0 to 10.0 on the detection of GR activity were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs alone changed slightly in the pH range of 4.0-10.0, indicating GQDs was very stable in such a pH range. While the enhancement factor (F-F 0)/F 0 increased with the increasing pH from 4.0 to 6.0 and then decreased with the increasing pH (Fig. 5B). Obviously, the pH 6.0 was the optimal one for probing the activity of GR.

Under the optimal conditions, we evaluated the capability of the GQDs-AuNPs based analysis system for the quantitative detection of GR. Fig. 6 (A) displays the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-AuNPs system in the presence of different concentrations of GR. As the GR levels increased from 0.0 to 0.13 mU/mL, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs-AuNPs system continually increased, implying a gradual biotransformation of GSSG to GSH catalyzed by GR. Fig. 6(B) presents the enhancement factor (F-F 0)/F 0 versus different concentrations of GR. The calibration curve was obtained for the GR in the range from 0.0050 to 0.13 mU/mL. The regression equation for the calibration curve for the calibration curve can be expressed as y = 0.0669 + 3.507 x, with R2 = 0.9874. This assay could allow for the detection of GR down to 0.0050 mU/mL as estimated from its calibration curve (S/N = 3). The commercially available kit for the DTNB colorimetric assay claims the concentration-dependent enzymatic reaction is linear between 6.76 mU/mL and 54.3 mU/mL, with a detection limit of 2.56 mU/mL. The results indicated that our fluorometric assay was 3 orders of magnitude more sensitive than the colorimetric assay. In addition, the detection limit of the proposed method is more than one order of magnitudes lower than most of those previous reports (Table 1 ).

It is widely established that an effective GR activity assay should be capable of monitoring the inhibition process. In our study, we examined a typical inhibitor 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU), which is the most commonly used irreversible GR inhibitor [45]. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system gradually decreased as the increasing concentrations of BCNU. These results indicated that the GR activity decreased with the increase in BCNU concentrations, illustrating the feasibility of the as-established assay for the screening of GR inhibitors.

We also examined the selectivity of this fluorescent GR sensor. The fluorescent responses of GQDs-AuNPs system to seven other proteins/enzymes were monitored and compared with that of GR, in which the experimental procedures were almost the same as the GR activity assay with the exception of GR being replaced by one of these proteins with ten times concentration of GR. As shown in Fig. 7 , negligible fluorescence changes were observed for these proteins, which is attributed to the high effective catalysis of GR toward GSSG in the presence of NADPH. During this study, we noticed that common small thiol-containing molecules like Cys and HCys also led to fluorescence change. They could be pretreated with the acrylamide through thiol-ene Michael addition reaction [46], so their existence has little influence upon the detection of GR (Fig. S7). Hence, this sensing platform can be applied as a selective nanosensor for GR determination without significant interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GR in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluated its applicability, the described method was applied to assay the GR activity in human serum samples. Human serum samples from one healthy volunteer was collected from Qufu People’s Hospital. In order to obtain a proper concentration range, the serum samples had been diluted 104 times before each experiment. Then, every 90 μL diluted serum sample were added into GQDs-AuNPs system for the following GR sensing. Table 2 showed the analysis results of GR activity in the spiked serum samples. According to the standard curve, the concentration of GR was calculated to be 538.8 mU/mL in the serum sample, which is consistent with the previous report [47]. In addition, the recoveries of the standard addition were measured between 98.4% and 109.4%, indicating the proposed method here was reproducible for detection of GR activity in the human serum samples.

The present work has introduced fluorescent GQDs as optical reporters for the biocatalytic transformations, which fluorescence could be quenched by AuNPs through FRET. The presence of GSH can protect AuNPs from being aggregated so as to trigger the recovery of GQDs fluorescence. Furthermore, the probing of biocatalytic enzyme-stimulated biotransformation was exemplified by using GR that can mediate the reduction of GSSG to yield GSH. Remarkably, GQDs can present some advantages over other fluorescent probes, such as low cost, easy preparation, and good photostability. Moreover, the longer emission wavelength of GQDs can avoid any interference of coenzyme NADPH. This proposed assay for probing GR activity is highly sensitive, sensitive, and environmentally friendly without any use of heavy metals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405094, 21775088, 21675099, and 21603116), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Nos. ZR2017MC037, ZR2016EMB04), the Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J16LA03), the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province of China (2016-ZJ-955), the Development Project of Qinghai Key Laboratory (No. 2017-ZJ-Y10), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (No. SKLEAC201506), and the Taishan Scholar Foundation of Shandong Province, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.096.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integration of photoswitchable proteins, photosynthetic reaction centers and semiconductor/biomolecule hybrids with electrode supports for optobioelectronic applications
Biosensing and probing of intracellular metabolic pathways by NADH-sensitive quantum dots
Carbon dot-assisted colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode protocol for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitors screening based on the inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles

Lab-on-a-drop: biocompatible fluorescent nanoprobesof gold nanoclusters for label-free evaluation of phosphorylation-induced inhibition of acetylcholinesterase activity towards the ultrasensitive detection of pesticide residues
Label-free fluorescent detection of protein kinase activity based on the aggregation behavior of unmodified quantum dots
Enzymatic growth of quantum dots: applications to probe glucose oxidase and horseradish peroxidase and sense glucose
Probing biocatalytic transformations with luminescent DNA/Silver nanoclusters
Graphene-quantum-dot assembled nanotubes: a new platform for efficient Raman enhancement
Ag nanoparticle-decorated graphene quantum dots for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Ag+ and biothiols
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Graphene oxide quantum dots@silver core-shell nanocrystals as turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen
A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots
A fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots-aptamer probe and graphene oxide platform for detection of lead (II) ion
Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury (II) ions
A boronic acid based glucose assay based on the suppression of the inner effect of gold nanoparticles on the orange fluorescence of graphene oxide quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid
Dopamine fluorescent sensors based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots core/shell hybrids
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
A universal fluorescence sensing strategy based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide for the detection of DNA
A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) biosensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for detection of mecA gene sequence of Staphylococcus aureus
Self-assembled graphene quantum dots induced by cytochrome c: a novel biosensor for trypsin with remarkable fluorescence enhancement
Enhanced graphene quantum dot fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive acetylcholinesterase assay and inhibitor screening
A fluorometric assay for tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening based on graphene quantum dots
Glyoxalase 1 and glutathione reductase regulate anxiety in inbred mouse strains
The histological distribution of glutathione reductase activity in solid mouse tumor transplants and a comparison with ascites tumors and normal tissues
Enzymic method for quantitative determination of nanogram amounts of total and oxidized glutathione
Assay for quantitative determination of glutathione and glutathione disulfide levels using enzymatic recycling method
Fluorometric assay for the determination of glutathione reductase activity
Fluorescence turn-on assay for glutathione reductase activity based on a conjugated polyelectrolyte with multiple carboxylate group
Enzymatic product-mediated stabilization of CdS quantum dots produced in situ: application for detection of reduced glutathione, NADPH, and glutathione reductase activity
Fluorescence detection of glutathione reductase activity based on deoxyribonucleic acid-templated silver nanoclusters
Preparation of novel fluorescent DNA bio-dots and their application for biothiols and glutathione reductase activity detection
Fluorimetric evaluation of glutathione reductase activity and its inhibitors using carbon quantum dots
Preparation and characterization of Au colloid systems
Design of gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric biosensing assays
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Multi-positively charged dendrimeric nanoparticles induced fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots for heparin and chondroitin sulfate detection
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive turn-on detection of glutathione and its cellular imaging
Amphiphilic egg-derived carbon dots: rapid plasma fabrication, pyrolysis process, and multicolor printing patterns
Anti-aggregation of gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensor for glutathione with excellent selectivity and sensitivity
Inhibition of human glutathione reductase by the nitrosourea drugs 1,3-bis (2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea and 1-(2-chloroethyl)-3-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-nitrosourea
Colorimetric detection of acrylamide in potato chips based on nucleophile-initiated thiol-ene Michael addition

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaolu Yan received her bachelor degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology from Qufu Normal University in 2016, and now is a postgraduate student in Analytical Chemistry at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests focus on the biosensors based on carbon nanomaterials and metal nanoclusters.

Xian-En Zhao received his PhD from Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology in 2009, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as an associate professor in Analytical Chemistry. His current research interests include biosensors, mass spectrometry analytical methodology, and pharmaceutical analysis.

Jing Sun received his PhD from Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology in 2007, and now is working at Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology as an associate professor in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources. Her current research interests include quality control of Tibetan medicine and chemical analysis of resources.

Shuyun Zhu received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry in 2012, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as an associate professor in Analytical Chemistry. Her current research interests include nanomaterials, as well as biosensors based on DNA and enzymes.

Cuihua Lei received her bachelor degree in Applied Chemistry from Weifang University in 2015, and now is a postgraduate student in Analytical Chemistry at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis and analytical application of carbon nanomaterials.

Ru Li received her bachelor degree in Chemical Education from Qufu Normal University in 2015, and now is a postgraduate student in Analytical Chemistry at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests focus on the optical sensors using metal ions as mimic enzyme.

Peiwei Gong received his PhD from Lanzhou Institute of Chemistry Physics in 2015, and now is working at Qufu Normal University. His current research interests include preparation of nano-materials and their applications in drug delivery and biosensors.

Baoping Ling received her PhD from Shandong University in 2010, and now is working at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests are theoretical studies on the enzymatic reactions mechanism, molecular dynamics simulations for biologic systems, as well as organometallic catalytic reactions.

Renjun Wang received her PhD from Ocean University of China in 2007, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as a professor in Marine Ecology. Her current research interests include the allelopathic effects of macroalgae on microalgae.

Hua Wang received his Ph.D from Hunan University in 2004, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as a distinguished professor in Chemistry. His research interests mainly include chemical and biological sensors, advanced materials, and organic synthesis.
